Paris 1874: The Impressionist Moment (The National Gallery)

In April 1874, a group of painters=E2=80=94Paul C=C3=A9zanne, Edgar Degas, =
Auguste Renoir, Claude Monet, Berthe Morisot, Camille Pissarro, and Alfred =
Sisley=E2=80=94mounted an exhibition outside of the control of the state-sp=
onsored Paris Salon, the source of work for most French artists in the 19th=
 century. The group, with others added later, would become known as the Imp=
ressionists.

It=E2=80=99s been 150 years since that radical break, and this show, which =
originated at Paris=E2=80=99s Mus=C3=A9e d=E2=80=99Orsay, is meant to empha=
size the rupture. Looking back after a century and a half, it can be hard t=
o remember that the Impressionists were once disruptors. By juxtaposing som=
e works featured in the official salon of the day with the often-disorienti=
ng images of modern life favored by the Impressionists, the show hopes to
dispel our image of Impressionism as simply intensely colored garden painti=
ngs.

Elizabeth Catlett: A Black Revolutionary Artist and All That It Implies (Br=
ooklyn Museum)

Born in 1915, the grandchild of slaves, Elizabeth Catlett graduated from Ho=
ward University and went on to study with Grant Wood, the bard of the Ameri=
can Midwest. In 1946, as artists were abandoning the social themes of the 1=
930s, Catlett accepted a fellowship to travel to Mexico City. She would wor=
k and live in Mexico for the rest of her life, though she returned to New Y=
ork often in her final decade.

Always a social realist, Catlett=E2=80=99s work was filled with explicit me=
ssages calling for social justice. It would take until the 1960s and =E2=80=
=9970s before there was a significant market for her work in the United Sta=
tes. The Brooklyn Museum show contains 150 prints and sculptures by the art=
ist.

Giorgio Morandi, Time Suspended II (Galleria Mattia de Luca in Manhattan)

A Roman gallerist is presenting a show of 60 works by Giorgio Morandi in a =
townhouse on New York=E2=80=99s Upper East Side. Curated by Marilena Pasqua=
li, founder and director of the Giorgio Morandi Study Center, Bologna, the =
show pulls together paintings and works on paper that span Morandi=E2=80=99=
s career from 1912 to 1964. Best known for his uncanny still lifes, the sho=
w, which opens on September 26, will also feature the artist=E2=80=99s land=
scape and flower paintings, as well as a number of works never before seen =
in New York. It=E2=80=99s not a museum show, but it might as well be.

k at the artist=E2=80=99s career as MoMA mounts what it calls =E2=80=9Cthe =
most comprehensive [exhibition] in the United States of Sch=C3=BCtte=E2=80=
=99s career,=E2=80=9D exploring =E2=80=9Cthe dazzling variety of his work a=
nd [locating] through lines that can connect a bunker to a bust.=E2=80=9D T=
he description is as inscrutable as much of Sch=C3=BCtte=E2=80=99s work, bu=
t now I=E2=80=99ll finally be able to better judge for myself.

st.html?utm_medium=3Dnative&utm_source=3Dpuck&utm_campaign=3Dwmnational_grp=

The Time Is Always Now: Artists Reframe the Black Figure (Philadelphia Muse=
um of Art)

Museums work slowly. We=E2=80=99re only now seeing the effects of the prote=
sts and upheaval of 2020 in museum programming, as three different shows ta=
ckling representation of Africa and the Black and African diaspora make the=
ir way to major museums in America. Ghana-born curator Ekow Eshun has assem=
bled works by Kerry James Marshall, Toyin Ojih Odutola, Amy Sherald, and Mi=
chael Armitage=E2=80=94all really talented artists=E2=80=94to address theme=
s such as W.E.B. Du Bois=E2=80=99s idea of double consciousness, the absenc=
e of Black figures in much mainstream art, and celebrations of Black assemb=
ly. The show opens on November 9.

Flight Into Egypt: Black Artist and Ancient Egypt, 1876-Now (The Met)

It won=E2=80=99t open until November, but the exhibition will feature 200 w=
orks of art that illustrate the ways that Black artists =E2=80=9Chave emplo=
yed Egyptian imagery to craft a unifying identity, the contributions of Bla=
ck scholars to the study of ancient Egypt, and the engagement of modern and=
 contemporary Egyptian artists with ancient Egypt.=E2=80=9D

Project a Black Planet: The Art and Culture of Panafrica (Art Institute of =
Chicago)

In December, Chicago will bring together 350 objects, from the 1920s to the=
 present, which explore the =E2=80=9Cemancipatory future=E2=80=9D of Pan-Af=
ricanism, =E2=80=9Ca shifting and boundless constellation that transforms a=
nd reassembles standard representation of the planet.=E2=80=9D The show fea=
tures work by Chris Ofili, Kerry James Marshall, David Hammons, Yto Barrada=
, and Simone Leigh.

ashing. Let=E2=80=99s all meet back here on Sunday,

             <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px;">
              <p style=3D"margin: 0; color: #000000; font-size: 16px; line-=
height: 30px; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 0;">Welcome back to Wall Pow=
er, my twice-weekly newsletter about how the art world <i>really</i> works.=
 I=E2=80=99m <b>Marion Maneker</b>. <br/><br/>On Sunday, I gave you my quic=
k read on the surprisingly sanguine mood at the art fairs held in New York =
last week. But art fairs are only one variable in the art market equation. =
Several important gallery shows opened last week, too, and more will open t=
his week. <br/><br/>I haven=E2=80=99t managed to hit many of them yet, but =
I did attend a press preview for <b>Mark Grotjahn</b>=E2=80=99s <i>Out of C=
ountry</i> paintings=E2=80=94a series of abstract art works partially inspi=
red by his adventures snowshoeing and skiing in the Rocky Mountains, where =
he has a home. It=E2=80=99s likely to be the <i>last</i> gallery show at <b=
>Larry Gagosian</b>=E2=80=99s flagship location at 980 Madison Avenue, acro=
ss from the Carlyle Hotel, before the main offices
are taken over by Bloomberg Philanthropies. Gagosian, who is said to have t=
ried to buy the Breuer Building (two blocks south) before it sold to Sotheb=
y=E2=80=99s, will look for new office space as he refurbishes the gallery=
=E2=80=99s exhibition and retail space on the street level. After all, he n=
eeds to preserve its proximity to Kappo Masa, the insanely expensive restau=
rant that he owns with chef <b>Masayoshi Takayama</b>.<br/><br/>Anyway, I=
=E2=80=99ll try to dig into the gallery shows around town another time. (Th=
ey will be up for six weeks or more.) Tonight, I want to look forward to so=
me of the museum exhibitions taking place around the Northeast (plus Chicag=
o) this fall=E2=80=94or at least those I want to see. This is only a partia=
l list and weighted toward my tastes. I=E2=80=99ll try to explain why. <br/=
></p>
             </td>

=94decided to take a walk for everyone=E2=80=99s benefit.) <br/><br/>Cue th=

therworldly refuge from contemporary life? <br/><br/>Anyway, Lowry clearly =

next phase

ied much of his tenure. As he himself has defined it, the museum needs to e=
ngage more with its community, and programming must become the next frontie=
r. That=E2=80=99s a job for someone with a vision for the future. It will b=
e exceedingly interesting to see whom they choose. </li><br/><li><i>Big mov=

 798 arts district. Later, the gallery opened a second location in Hong Kon=

ead sales, business development, and artist engagement in the region=E2=80=
=9D beginning in October. Pace already has a show of <b>Robert
Indiana</b>=E2=80=99s work scheduled for March 2025 to coincide with Art Ba=
sel=E2=80=99s opening in Hong Kong. <br/><br/>Lin says her goal is to conti=
nue educating Chinese and Asian buyers about Western art. After all, there =
are <i>still</i> many new collectors who understand Asian art but don=E2=80=
=99t know much about related Western artists. In the case of Robert Indiana=
, for example, they=E2=80=99ll be familiar with his <i>Love</i> sculpture b=
ut not much else. Therein lies the economic opportunity=E2=80=94to provide =
that context, and a narrative that can enrapture wealthy Asian collectors. =
</li><br/><li><i>C.J. Hendry opens on Roosevelt Island</i>: There=E2=80=99s=
 a fine line between art and merchandise. Instead of worrying about crossin=
g that boundary, <b>C.J. Hendry</b> has made it her muse. For her latest ac=

=80=9D to Roosevelt Island=E2=80=99s Four Freedoms State Park from Septembe=
r 13 to 15, in collaboration with beauty brand Cl=C3=A9 de Peau. The
elaborate drop consists of plush flowers ($5) and merchandise (silk scarves=

 of Hendry=E2=80=99s photorealistic art work. (The images, drawn on paper, =
will go for $22,900.) As with all of Hendry=E2=80=99s events, there will be=
 a pop-up flower market open through the weekend. </li><br/><li><i>Hauser &=
amp; Wirth to (co-)represent Michaela Yearwood-Dan</i>: Outsiders might exp=
ress disappointment with the market for <b>Michaela Yearwood-Dan</b>, whose=
 auction prices peaked in early 2023 at more than $800,000. Since then, nea=
rly a dozen works have been auctioned at prices between $200,000 and $500,0=
00. The reality, of course, is that this is simply how the art market works=
=2E Indeed, with the strong hand of Man=
hattan art dealer <b>Marianne Boesky</=
b> guiding her sales, Yearwood-Dan will be extending her reach through Haus=
er & Wirth=E2=80=99s broad
international network of galleries. Starting in October, Yearwood-Dan will =

             <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: =
16px; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">In April 1874, a=
 group of painters=E2=80=94<b>Paul C=C3=A9zanne</b>, <b>Edgar Degas</b>, <b=
>Auguste Renoir</b>, <b>Claude Monet</b>, <b>Berthe Morisot</b>, <b>Camille=
 Pissarro, </b>and <b>Alfred Sisley</b>=E2=80=94mounted an exhibition outsi=
de of the control of the state-sponsored Paris Salon, the source of work fo=
r most French artists in the 19th century. The group, with others added lat=
er, would become known as the Impressionists. <br/><br/>It=E2=80=99s been 1=
50 years since that radical break, and this show, which originated at Paris=
=E2=80=99s Mus=C3=A9e d=E2=80=99Orsay, is meant to emphasize the rupture. L=
ooking back after a century and a half, it can be hard to remember that the=
 Impressionists were once disruptors. By juxtaposing some works featured in=
 the official salon of the day with the often-disorienting images of modern=
 life favored by the Impressionists, the show hopes to
dispel our image of Impressionism as simply intensely colored garden painti=
ngs. <br/></td>
            </tr>

             <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: =
16px; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Born in 1915, th=
e grandchild of slaves, Elizabeth Catlett graduated from Howard University =
and went on to study with <b>Grant Wood</b>, the bard of the American Midwe=
st. In 1946, as artists were abandoning the social themes of the 1930s, Cat=
lett accepted a fellowship to travel to Mexico City. She would work and liv=
e in Mexico for the rest of her life, though she returned to New York often=
 in her final decade. <br/><br/>Always a social realist, Catlett=E2=80=99s =
work was filled with explicit messages calling for social justice. It would=
 take until the 1960s and =E2=80=9970s before there was a significant marke=
t for her work in the United States. The Brooklyn Museum show contains 150 =
prints and sculptures by the artist. <br/></td>
            </tr>
